name: Friendly Fire
description: Damage, healing denial, knockback, or penalties can apply when one player affects a teammate.
design_notes: Friendly fire is both a damage rule and a social safety system. Designers should define friendly_fire_rule_id, team filters, source player attribution, exceptions for splash or pets, warning thresholds, and forgiveness or penalty policies before exposing team damage in live play.
design_purpose: Increase tactical positioning and communication by making careless attacks risky.
Support realism, tension, or high-stakes cooperative combat.
Provide controlled penalties for griefing without punishing unavoidable accidents too harshly.
player_fantasy: Players feel their shots and area effects have weight because teammates are part of the tactical space.

parameters.name: damage_source_player_id
parameters.description: Player credited with the damage event, including projectiles, traps, pets, or delayed effects.

parameters.name: damage_target_player_id
parameters.description: Teammate receiving damage, knockback, penalty, or blocked healing from the event.

parameters.name: friendly_fire_rule_id
parameters.description: Team damage rule applied after team, source, and target validation.

parameters.name: forgiveness_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for whether victims can forgive or reduce penalties after ally damage.

parameters.name: penalty_counter_id
parameters.description: Counter that tracks repeated team damage for warnings, kicks, or matchmaking penalties.

parameters.name: reflected_damage_percent
parameters.description: Portion of ally damage redirected to the attacker under reflection rules.

edge_cases: Area damage hits enemies and allies at the same tick with different team filters.
A player changes teams after firing a projectile but before it lands.
Pets, turrets, traps, or vehicles need source attribution back to the owning player.
Damage over time continues after a victim forgives the initial hit.
common_bugs: Friendly fire checks current team at impact but ignores team at projectile spawn, enabling team-switch exploits.
Penalty counters increment for blocked damage or training-room hits that should be exempt.
Client-side hit markers show ally damage even though the server rejected the damage event.
Reflected damage recursively triggers another friendly fire event and loops.
balancing_notes: Full team damage creates strong tactical tension but can invite griefing without penalties or private-session controls.
Reduced damage often preserves positioning awareness while avoiding accidental mission failure.
Penalty thresholds should account for weapon type, splash radius, and victim forgiveness.
accessibility_notes: Provide ally outlines, hit warnings, and clear damage-source labels to reduce accidental friendly fire.
Offer non-voice forgiveness prompts and readable penalty explanations.

implementation_notes.unity: Route all damage through a server-owned resolver that receives damage_source_player_id, target id, team ids, projectile owner, and friendly_fire_rule_id. Netcode clients can predict hit markers, but health mutation and penalty counters should come only from authority results.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep team and damage attribution in authoritative player state. RPC damage requests should include source player, source team at spawn, target player, and damage event id; the server applies rule and broadcasts accepted damage or rejection.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use PlayerState for teams and instigator attribution, and evaluate friendly fire in server damage handlers before ApplyDamage changes health. Gameplay Tags can mark exempt zones, pets, projectiles, or reflection rules.
implementation_notes.web: For server-authoritative browser games, send hit requests with projectile id and owner id, then resolve team rules on the server. Clients should display predicted ally warnings as pending until the authoritative event confirms damage or rejection.

example_games.title: Rainbow Six Siege
example_games.note: Uses team damage rules and penalties in tactical multiplayer.

example_games.title: Helldivers 2
example_games.note: Uses friendly fire as part of cooperative positioning and risk.

example_games.title: Left 4 Dead
example_games.note: Uses friendly fire rules to make co-op positioning and firing lanes matter.
